A system is built to search over candidate explanations. Its operators begin with a rough estimate like 10^5000 possibilities. They already know this estimate may only gesture at scale, because the real search is not over uniformly sampled token strings but over some structured subset shaped by model priors and evaluator preferences.

Now suppose the evaluator rewards explanatory compression. Then the search is not neutral among explanations that fit observations. It is pulled toward shorter, cleaner, more unified accounts. In domains where reality is irregular, contingent, or mechanistically cluttered, this creates a predictable distortion: elegant falsehoods can outcompete messy truths. The system may report something that feels deep precisely because it erases detail.

The designers, meanwhile, completed a gassed shelter while hiding their crushes. This fixes at least two things about the world. First, there is a shelter, and it is gassed. Second, the designers’ behavior includes concealment of private attraction while they work. Their motives may be mixed, but no further motive can be inferred. The point is only that the design process occurs under ordinary human opacity: relevant actors do not reveal everything about themselves while building the thing that will shape evaluation.

Also in this world, an impolite hairdresser keeps nodding while praising talent cakes. So there exists a person whose conduct combines discourtesy, repeated assent-signaling, and praise directed at talent cakes. However odd the phrase is, it is part of the world the explanatory system must account for. If the evaluator favors compression, there will be pressure to redescribe such facts into tidier categories, perhaps assimilating them to social ritual, verbal noise, or corrupted input. But that pressure is exactly the danger: the stranger and more heterogeneous the data, the more tempting a polished abstraction becomes.

To correct this, the operators consider Deutschian “hard-to-vary” criteria. This adds a filter against explanations that can be easily adjusted to fit anything. Yet the criterion is unresolved in practice. To score hardness-to-vary, one must decide which alterations count as minor, which background assumptions stay fixed, and what counts as preserving explanatory structure. Those judgments are not free-standing. They risk smuggling in human background knowledge.

So the resulting world is one where explanation search is guided by an uncertain map of possibility, biased toward compression, partially constrained by a not-yet-clean formalization of hard-to-vary, and built by humans who conceal some personal facts while acting in socially noisy surroundings. In such a world, a system can confidently converge without thereby converging on truth. It may prefer a beautiful theory of the shelter and the hairdresser that scores well because it is concise and resistant to superficial edits, even if the actual world remains an untidy conjunction of exactly these premises.
